So Parkinson's disease is characterized or comes about because of a loss of those dopamine producing cells within the basal ganglia circuitry.
First I just want to show you what some of the patients look like with Parkinson's disease.
This is a gentleman trying to walk and what you can see is he gets really stuck.
He's trying to just walk across the room, but his feet get stuck, can't quite get the movement going, he's kind of hunched over a little bit and now he gets going again.
Very slow and these individuals have great trouble with movement transitions.

He's actually kicking that cane as he walks across the room.
He actually has pretty good balance. He doesn't need the cane for balance.

He's come up with this trick that is if he has that cane out there, he uses that as a visual cue and basically to get himself to be able to walk, he uses that visual cue to be able to kick that cane as he goes across the room and this is something known with Parkinson's disease that they have a lot of trouble initiating a movement internally if they just have to do it through their internal will, but if it's something that actually has sort of an external cue, they can do much better.
So he's come up with a solution to kick the cane.

I worked with another patient who actually wore a little device on his head that flashed an LED down on the ground in front of him and he sort of found that he could facilitate his walking by trying to chase that dot.

If you've ever seen the movie Awakenings with Robin Williams, there's a scene in there where this is before they've actually discovered the medication we'll talk about to treat Parkinson's disease and these were people who are very frozen.

They really are unable to make almost any movement at all, but there's one scene in the movement where Robin Williams throws a ball at one of the patients and he reaches up with his hand and grabs it.

So if there's a very salient visual signal or external signal, it really can get the patients to move, but on their own, there's internal sort of generation of movement is what's severely affected by the disorder.

Here's a healthy brain and you see this dark band in here and the substantia nigra, these dopamine cells, have actually iron in them and that's what gives them this dark appearance.

And this is just showing that in a PD brain, okay, here's the healthy brain again, here's the PD brain, and you can see that there's been severe atrophy or loss of these iron-rich dopamine-producing cells.
There is some genetic propensity for Parkinson's, but it's very low.
It's like maybe about one to three percent of all the cases have potentially some genetic component to them.
Really, people suspect that whatever causes Parkinson's disease might be some environmental toxin that people are exposed to.
Lots of interesting research saying that Parkinson's disease is higher in farming communities, which makes people think that maybe exposure to pesticides.
An interesting paper came out a few years ago saying that actually being infected with the flu increases the risk of Parkinson's disease.
This is kind of showing data about odds ratio means what's the greater likelihood.
So two times odds ratio means you have twice as likely a chance compared to the normal population to get Parkinson's disease.
It's showing that cumulatively, over your lifetime, how the risk increases as a function of the number of times you've actually experienced the flu.

And there's also work suggesting that traumatic brain injury might accelerate the death of these cells, and consequently Parkinson's disease can be quite high, say, in populations that have a lot of TBI, such as boxers, Muhammad Ali being a famous case there of someone with very advanced Parkinson's disease.
Well, again, here is that complicated picture I showed you there.
Let's just, again, forget about the indirect pathway and just focus on the direct pathway.
Remember I said in the direct pathway, the dopamine cells are amplifying the activity in the direct pathway because they're excitatory.
So if I take out those cells, pretend I have complete loss of the dopamine cells in the substantia nigra, well, if I'm reducing excitation of the direct pathway, what happens?
The direct pathway... direct pathway is weakened, so I have reduced inhibition from the striatum to the globus pallidus.
If I've reduced inhibition, that means that the output from the globus pallidus to the thalamus is increased, and so I now have increased inhibition of the thalamus.
Increased inhibition of the thalamus means I have reduced excitation of the cerebral cortex.

So again, the primary consequence we can see, especially on this direct pathway, is if I lose the dopamine cells in the substantia nigra, I end up with much weaker excitation of the cortex from the subcortex.
I have increased inhibition, basically, of the cortex. So how would I think about when I called akinesia is a loss of movement or bradykinesia, slowing of movement?
The two principal symptoms we see in Parkinson's disease. So akinesia means that they just produce much fewer movements than healthy individuals.
Bradykinesia refers to the fact that, well, their movements are really slow, even when they produce them, they're quite slow.
And if we think back to that model I showed you of the basal ganglia as a dam or as a gate on the cerebral cortex, you might think about Parkinson's disease as a stuck gate.
And the idea here is that, here, think of this red line as a one activation pattern that's gonna reach threshold, that's gonna be a selected movement.
So for instance, in that baby in the milk bottle example, this might be the activation function for the possible movement of, you know, use my head to pick up the water bottle.
Usually, again, you might think that in a healthy brain, there's sort of a threshold, how much activation do I need to open up the gate?
But the idea here is that if I've lost that dopamine, that threshold is much higher.

So now, even if the cortex is working properly, it's showing the same activation function, I need much stronger activation from the cortex in order to initiate the movement, in order to break through that dam.
And again, think back to that example I gave you of, of, you know, in the movie Awakenings about throwing the ball in the patient can do it.
The notion there is that if you have the salient stimulus coming at your face, then the activation function might be much steeper, you might have much stronger activation function.

And so it's gonna actually quickly reach this elevated threshold that we might think about as a way to characterize what the brain's like or how the basal ganglia is functioning in people with Parkinson's disease.

They need to have something like the cane boosting that activation to initiate the movement.

Premotor regions are kind of translating goals into motor codes, especially for more complex movements.

And now I'm putting in the basal ganglia here as this, what I'm calling a dumb mechanism that's gonna help resolve the competition.

The cortex is generating all the possibilities. The basal ganglia is playing this function in this dumb way, not really thinking about the contents, but just saying, is one particular movement sufficiently activated?

If so, I'm going to initiate that movement. Well, thinking about things this way, we can now think about all the different therapies that have been developed over the last, we're now talking about 75 years or so in the treatment of Parkinson's disease.
So pre 1950, mid 1950s, person got Parkinson's disease and they just got slower and slower, less and less movement.
They eventually would die from Parkinson's disease because basically the same thing that's happening with their volitional movements is also happening to their autonomic nervous system.
And eventually they have difficulty breathing. In the 1950s though, people came up with the idea of knowing that dopamine was gone.
Could they actually produce a synthetic version of dopamine? That's what's called L-Dopa therapy.
It stands for levodopa. And this is really one of the great miracles of the 20th century medicine.

Okay. Basically L-Dopa is a medication. It's not going to actually store the cells in the stanchionigra, but it's actually a synthetic version of dopamine that's going to activate those dopamine receptors in the striatum.
And so here we have a synthetic. medication for a way to actually reactivate those dopamine cells and the effects were just dramatic.
People went from being frozen to suddenly being able to move again.

And you see that again, watch the movie Awakenings. It's called Awakenings because these people are frozen or some of the early trial individuals who are getting the L-Dopa therapy and they go from being frozen to being able to move.
And basically L-Dopa therapy is the first line of treatment in everyone with Parkinson's disease.
Almost all patients have a positive response to L-Dopa therapy.
They really are able to move much better. It can take quite a while to actually regulate the L-Dopa therapy to find what's the level of therapy that's appropriate for an individual.
But they certainly go from this frozen state to being able to move again.
And again the idea is, as I add here, the idea is that basically you might think about that medication is in a sense restoring that threshold.
It's kind of making it so that we no longer need extra cortical activation, but we can move the patient's threshold back down towards the normal level.
Now the problem though became apparent after about 20 or 30 years in the 1970s and 80s.
It started becoming recognized that there were some problems with long-term use of L-Dopa therapy.
First of all, as with many medications, the efficacy of the drug changed over the years.

It became less effective. And so of course the kind of intervention someone might try is, well if the drugs that I was giving at one level aren't effective as they were, much as effective as they were in the past, let's increase the drug medication so it might tinker with how much drug the person's taking.
But also what became apparent is that actually over the years patients started developing hyper-movement problems.
They became what we call dyskinetic or having excessive movements.

And it's actually going to be a problem that starts to become apparent in about 50% of the patients after they've been on L-Dopa therapy for five years and it becomes quite pronounced in some individuals.

I can't really think clearly. So a lot of interest about, you know, is Parkinson's disease also slowing down cognition in a sense?
That's what this gentleman was saying, is that basically his cognition just felt in a muddle if he didn't take his medication.
